{"step_by_step_thinking": "The relevant information can be found in Document [21] (Title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development). It states that the odontogenesis of permanent teeth begins in the twentieth week of embryonic development. Given that the twenty-week mark is approximately 4.75 months into the intrauterine life, the first permanent molars would begin to calcify after this point, ruling out option A. Option B is incorrect as the teeth do not begin calcification at birth. Option C is also incorrect as the deciduous incisors begin to erupt around 6-10 months of age, which is after the beginning of calcification of the permanent molars. Option D is the correct answer as the twenty-week mark is roughly about 1 year after conception, which aligns with the statement that the first permanent molars begin to calcify about 1 year after birth.", "answer_choice": "D"}
